title: Twelve technical, two real
summary: An old composer reviews his apprentice's score and explains the difference between the kind of error he can fix and the kind only she can.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Sarah, sit. I have read your score.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: And?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: The first movement has fourteen errors. Twelve are technical. Two are real.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Which are which?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Most students cannot tell. The technical ones are obvious — a forbidden parallel fifth, an unprepared dissonance, a horn line that goes below its range. We can fix them in an afternoon. The real errors take longer.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: What is a real error, then?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: A real error is a moment when the music does what you want, exactly, and you wanted the wrong thing.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Show me one.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Bar forty-seven. The strings climb a perfect fourth, the oboe joins, the timpani enters precisely on the bar line. It works. It is technically clean. But the strings should not have arrived.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Why not?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Because in bar forty-six, the cello has just played a phrase that asks a question. The audience needs three beats to hear that question. In bar forty-seven, you answered it. You answered too fast. The orchestra is now talking over a question your own cello asked. Technically correct. Musically rude.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: I didn't notice.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: You will notice. That is what students confuse. They think the work is fixing parallel fifths. The work is hearing rudeness.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Will you help me fix bar forty-seven?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: I will not. You will sit with it. I want you to play bar forty-six on a piano, twenty times. Then ask: did anyone want to listen after that? Three beats of silence, or a single oboe line, or the cello answering itself. Many solutions. None of them require me.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: The other technical errors —

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: I will mark them in pencil. They are routine.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Maestro, what is the second real error?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Bar one hundred and twelve. The piece resolves. It returns to the home key. It is satisfying.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Why is that an error?

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Because the piece is about a woman who never resolves. You wrote a piece about an unresolved life and ended it in C major. You gave her an ending she would not have accepted.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: I see.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Now do you understand the difference?

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: I think I do.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: One last thing. The technical errors I will help you fix. The real errors I will not, because I do not know that woman. You do. She is in the piece because you put her there. You are the only one who can decide what ending she does or does not deserve.

body.speaker: Sarah
body.text: Thank you, Maestro.

body.speaker: Vermeer
body.text: Come back next Tuesday. With a corrected bar forty-seven, or with three reasons why no correction is possible.

annotations.meaningVi: bản tổng phổ (bản nhạc cho dàn nhạc)
quãng năm song song (một lỗi 'truyền thống' trong hoà âm cổ điển)
(âm nhạc) giải quyết — trở về hợp âm chủ
giọng chủ (giọng nền của bản nhạc)

grammarNotes.title: 'Should have' and 'would not have' for evaluative critique
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Để phê bình một lựa chọn đã thực hiện trong quá khứ, B2 dùng 'should have + V3' (đáng lẽ phải) và 'would not have + V3' (sẽ không). Trong bài: 'The strings should not have arrived.' (đáng lẽ chúng không nên xuất hiện ở chỗ đó). 'You gave her an ending she would not have accepted.' (nhân vật giả định sẽ không chấp nhận). Hai cấu trúc này cho phép thầy đánh giá lựa chọn nghệ thuật như đánh giá quyết định đạo đức — có thể được phán xét sau khi đã xảy ra.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To critique a past choice, B2 uses 'should have + past participle' (ought to have done) and 'would not have + past participle' (hypothetically wouldn't). The passage: 'The strings should not have arrived.' (they shouldn't have appeared there). 'You gave her an ending she would not have accepted.' (a fictional character is treated as if she had agency to refuse). These structures let an artistic choice be judged after the fact, the way a moral decision can be.

translationVi:
Vermeer: Sarah, ngồi đi. Tôi đã đọc bản tổng phổ của em.
Sarah: Và sao ạ?
Vermeer: Chương một có mười bốn lỗi. Mười hai là lỗi kỹ thuật. Hai là lỗi thật.
Sarah: Lỗi nào là lỗi nào?
Vermeer: Đa số sinh viên không phân biệt được. Lỗi kỹ thuật hiển nhiên — một quãng năm song song cấm, một dissonance không chuẩn bị, một dòng kèn vượt quá tầm. Trong một chiều là sửa xong. Lỗi thật mất lâu hơn.
Sarah: Vậy lỗi thật là gì?
Vermeer: Lỗi thật là khoảnh khắc mà âm nhạc làm đúng điều em muốn — và điều em muốn lại là sai.
Sarah: Cho em xem một lỗi.
Vermeer: Ô nhịp bốn mươi bảy. Đàn dây trèo lên một quãng bốn, kèn oboe vào, trống timpani đánh đúng vạch nhịp. Nó hoạt động. Kỹ thuật sạch. Nhưng đàn dây đáng lẽ không nên xuất hiện.
Sarah: Sao thế ạ?
Vermeer: Vì ở ô bốn sáu, cello vừa chơi một câu nhạc đặt ra một câu hỏi. Khán giả cần ba phách để nghe được câu hỏi đó. Đến ô bốn bảy thì em đã trả lời. Em trả lời quá nhanh. Cả dàn nhạc đang nói chồng lên một câu hỏi mà chính cello của em vừa hỏi. Đúng kỹ thuật. Thô lỗ về âm nhạc.
Sarah: Em không nhận ra.
Vermeer: Em sẽ nhận ra. Đó là cái sinh viên hay nhầm. Họ tưởng công việc là sửa quãng năm song song. Công việc là nghe sự thô lỗ.
Sarah: Maestro giúp em sửa ô bốn bảy không ạ?
Vermeer: Tôi sẽ không. Em sẽ ngồi với nó. Tôi muốn em chơi ô bốn sáu trên piano hai mươi lần. Rồi tự hỏi: có ai còn muốn nghe sau đó không? Ba phách im lặng, một dòng oboe, hay cello tự trả lời. Có nhiều giải pháp. Không cái nào cần tôi.
Sarah: Còn các lỗi kỹ thuật khác —
Vermeer: Tôi sẽ đánh dấu bút chì. Chuyện thường.
Sarah: Thưa thầy, lỗi thật thứ hai là gì?
Vermeer: Ô một trăm mười hai. Bản nhạc kết thúc giải. Trở về giọng chủ. Đã thoả mãn.
Sarah: Sao đó lại là lỗi?
Vermeer: Vì bản nhạc nói về một người phụ nữ chưa bao giờ được 'giải'. Em viết một bản nhạc về một cuộc đời chưa được giải và kết nó ở Đô trưởng. Em đã cho cô ấy một kết thúc mà cô ấy sẽ không chấp nhận.
Sarah: Em hiểu.
Vermeer: Giờ em hiểu khác biệt chưa?
Sarah: Em nghĩ em hiểu rồi.
Vermeer: Một điều cuối. Lỗi kỹ thuật, tôi sẽ giúp em sửa. Lỗi thật thì không, vì tôi không quen người phụ nữ đó. Em quen. Cô ấy có mặt trong bản nhạc là vì em đặt cô ấy ở đó. Em là người duy nhất có thể quyết định kết thúc nào cô ấy xứng đáng — hoặc không xứng đáng — nhận.
Sarah: Cảm ơn thầy.
Vermeer: Quay lại thứ Ba tới. Mang theo ô bốn bảy đã sửa, hoặc ba lý do vì sao không thể sửa.

questions.prompt: How many errors does Vermeer find in the first movement?
options: Twelve technical and two real (fourteen in total)
questions.explanation: 'The first movement has fourteen errors. Twelve are technical. Two are real.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is given as an example of a technical error?
options: A character ending in the wrong key
A cello question answered too fast
questions.explanation: 'A forbidden parallel fifth, an unprepared dissonance, a horn line that goes below its range.'
questions.hint: Vermeer's first long answer about technical errors.

questions.prompt: How does Vermeer define a 'real error'?
options: A moment when the music does what you want, exactly, and you wanted the wrong thing.
A horn line out of range.
questions.hint: Look at his definition of a real error.

questions.prompt: What is the problem at bar forty-seven?
options: The strings, oboe, and timpani arrive 'cleanly' but interrupt a question the cello has just asked — they answer too fast.
The horn plays out of range.
The cello is out of tune.
questions.hint: Look at Vermeer's diagnosis of bars 46 and 47.

questions.prompt: How does Vermeer describe the bar-47 mistake in one phrase?
questions.hint: Look at the two short final sentences in his bar-47 speech.

questions.prompt: Why won't Vermeer fix bar forty-seven himself?
options: Because he wants Sarah to sit with it — play bar 46 on a piano twenty times, then ask whether anyone wanted to listen.
Because he disagrees with the whole piece.
questions.explanation: 'You will sit with it. I want you to play bar forty-six on a piano, twenty times. Then ask: did anyone want to listen after that?'

questions.prompt: What does he say students confuse about the work?
options: They think the work is fixing parallel fifths; in fact the work is hearing rudeness.
They think the work is listening; in fact it is composing.
They think parallel fifths are allowed; in fact they are not.
They think rudeness is the entire job.
questions.explanation: 'They think the work is fixing parallel fifths. The work is hearing rudeness.'
questions.hint: Look at his short, contrastive line.

questions.prompt: What is the second real error?
options: Bar 112 — the piece resolves to the home key (C major), but the piece is about a woman who never resolves.
Bar 112 — the piece does not resolve and should.
questions.explanation: 'The piece is about a woman who never resolves. You wrote a piece about an unresolved life and ended it in C major.'

questions.prompt: How does he phrase the wrongness of the resolution?
options: 'You gave her an ending she would not have accepted.'
questions.hint: Look at his sentence about the ending.

questions.prompt: Why won't he fix the real errors for Sarah?
options: Because he does not know the woman the piece is about. Sarah put her there; only she can decide what ending the woman does or does not deserve.
Because she did not pay enough tuition.
Because he is leaving the country.
questions.explanation: 'The real errors I will not, because I do not know that woman. You do. She is in the piece because you put her there.'
questions.hint: Look at his last long speech.

questions.prompt: What is Sarah's homework for next Tuesday?
options: A corrected bar 47, or three reasons why no correction is possible
A list of all parallel fifths
questions.explanation: 'With a corrected bar forty-seven, or with three reasons why no correction is possible.'

questions.prompt: Which of these is the dialogue NOT claiming?
options: That technical mastery alone determines the quality of a score.
That a piece can be technically clean yet musically rude.
That some errors only the composer can fix.
That a teacher can refuse to help on principle.
questions.explanation: Vermeer explicitly distinguishes 'technically correct' from 'real' — technical mastery is not enough.

questions.prompt: What does the maestro do with the other technical errors?
options: He marks them in pencil — they are routine.
He refuses to look at them.
questions.explanation: 'I will mark them in pencil. They are routine.'

questions.prompt: What does Vermeer's 'hearing rudeness' mean here?
options: Listening for rude language at a concert
Noticing when a musically clean passage disrespects what came just before it
questions.explanation: He uses 'rude' for the orchestra cutting off the cello's question.
questions.hint: Match his term to his example.

questions.prompt: What is the main idea of the dialogue?
options: Real errors in creative work are decisions about what the work ought to mean — and only the maker can resolve them; a teacher can fix technique, not intent.
Composing is mostly avoiding parallel fifths.
Apprentices should always do as their teachers say.
C major is the wrong key for any modern piece.
questions.explanation: Vermeer separates fixable technique from the deeper question of what the music should be doing, which belongs to the composer.
questions.hint: Notice what Vermeer refuses to fix, and why.

cloze.template:
Vermeer: Sarah, sit. I have read your score.
Sarah: And?
Vermeer: The first movement has fourteen errors. Twelve are {{b1}}. Two are real.
Sarah: Which are which?
Vermeer: Most students cannot tell. The technical ones are obvious — a forbidden parallel {{b2}}, an unprepared dissonance, a horn line that goes below its range. We can fix them in an afternoon. The real errors take longer.
Sarah: What is a real error, then?
Vermeer: A real error is a moment when the music does what you want, exactly, and you wanted the {{b3}} thing.
Sarah: Show me one.
Vermeer: Bar forty-seven. The strings climb a perfect fourth, the oboe joins, the timpani enters precisely on the bar line. It works. It is technically clean. But the strings should not have {{b4}}.
Sarah: Why not?
Vermeer: Because in bar forty-six, the cello has just played a phrase that asks a {{b5}}. The audience needs three beats to hear that question. In bar forty-seven, you {{b6}} it. You answered too fast. The orchestra is now talking over a question your own cello asked. Technically correct. Musically {{b7}}.
Sarah: I didn't notice.
Vermeer: You will notice. That is what students confuse. They think the work is fixing parallel fifths. The work is hearing {{b8}}.
Sarah: Will you help me fix bar forty-seven?
Vermeer: I will not. You will sit with it. I want you to play bar forty-six on a piano, {{b9}} times. Then ask: did anyone want to listen after that? Three beats of silence, or a single oboe line, or the cello answering itself. Many solutions. None of them require me.
Sarah: The other technical errors —
Vermeer: I will mark them in {{b10}}. They are routine.
Sarah: Maestro, what is the second real error?
Vermeer: Bar one hundred and twelve. The piece resolves. It returns to the home key. It is satisfying.
Sarah: Why is that an error?
Vermeer: Because the piece is about a woman who never resolves. You wrote a piece about an unresolved life and ended it in {{b11}} major. You gave her an ending she would not have {{b12}}.
Sarah: I see.
Vermeer: Now do you understand the difference?
Sarah: I think I do.
Vermeer: One last thing. The technical errors I will help you fix. The real errors I will not, because I do not know that {{b13}}. You do. She is in the piece because you put her there. You are the only one who can decide what ending she does or does not {{b14}}.
Sarah: Thank you, Maestro.
Vermeer: Come back next Tuesday. With a corrected bar forty-seven, or with three {{b15}} why no correction is possible.

blanks.explanation: The strings should not have arrived.
A phrase that asks a question.
She would not have accepted it.
I do not know that woman.
What ending she does or does not deserve.
Three reasons why no correction is possible.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Vermeer separates errors a teacher can fix from errors only the creator can. Is there a piece of work you have done — a project, a piece of writing, a relationship — where most criticism you received was technical, but you suspect the real problem was about intent? Why is the real problem so much harder to name out loud?
